entries.question: Could you please provide me with the tariff for the following code in the cases for COIDA patients: 6260 Contrast Medium: Current price according to the regular price list published by the Radiological Society of SA
entries.answer: Code 6260 is the 4 digit COIDA radiology contrast code used by specialist radiologists to bill for MRI contrast media (if and when applicable to a MRI procedure). There are various suppliers of the MRI contrast in South Africa and contrast prices per supplier vary. Each supplier has their own NAPPI code registered for their specific contrast product (i.e. various contrast NAPPI codes [with different prices] can be billed for under code 6260) The NAPPI code prices for radiology consumables and contrast are being calculated and published by a company called Medprax. You can contac Medprax at: Medprax (Pty) Ltd. Tel: +27 31 904 9200 Direct line: +27 31 904 9208 Fax: +27 31 904 9211 karen@medprax.co.za http://www.medprax.co.za

entries.question: 5 Digit Coding Explanation
entries.answer: Below and attached (original introduction files) to use for explanation of the 5 digit coding structure Anatomical hierarchy, followed by modality hierarchy 1st digit – main anatomical region 2nd digit – sub region 3rd digit – modality descriptor 4th and 5th – procedure specific Trevor Radiological Society of South Africa Guidelines to the RSSA Radiology Coding Structure Version 3: 26 Sept 2003 The coding structure is comprehensive, covering all imaging modalities and is for the exclusive use of registered specialist radiology practices (PCNS “038”) and nuclear medicine practices (PCNS “025”) Code Structure Framework a. The tariff code consists of 5 digits i. 1st digit indicates the main anatomical region or procedural category. • 0 = General (non specific) • 1 = Head • 2 = Neck • 3 = Thorax • 4 = Abdomen and Pelvis (soft tissue) • 5 = Spine, Pelvis and Hips • 6 = Upper limbs • 7 = Lower limbs • 8 = Interventional • 9 = Soft tissue regions (nuclear medicine) • eg “Head” = 1xxxx i. 2nd digit indicates the sub region within a main region or category eg. • “Head / Skull and Brain” = 10xxx ii. 3rd digit indicates modality • 1 = General (Black and White) x-rays • 2 = Ultrasound • 3 = Computed Tomography • 4 = Magnetic Resonance Imaging • 5 = Angiography • 6 = Interventional radiology • 9 = Nuclear Medicine (Isotopes) eg: “Head / Skull and Brain / General x-ray” = 101xx iii. 4th and 5th digits are specific to a procedure / examination, eg “Head / Skull and Brain / General / X-ray of the skull” = 10100. Guidelines for use of coding structure • The vast majority of the codes describe complete procedures / examination and their use for the appropriate studies is self-explanatory.

entries.question: We just had a disgruntled patient (he is employed by the Dept of trade & industry) that demanded to know which act allow us to charge an Admin fee and he is going to lay a formal charge against us. Please advise
entries.answer: After the 2010 court case there is no NRPL or benchmark for billing and the schemes determine their own benefit rates. This includes codes against which they pay and the amount they pay. The schemes are both entitled to reject a code as being outside their benefits and classify an account containing a code, for which they do not provide benefits as an 'out of benefit account’. Further, and our previous emails refer to this, under the National Health Act you are obligated to tell the patient the cost of an examination before it is done. There is no obligation to tell a patient whether or not a scheme will cover the amount charged as a practitioner cannot be reasonable expected to know the benefit options for every patient and every scheme option. However if you charge a code which you know will not be paid by the majority of schemes it would be expected that you inform the patient of this. So in this specific case ther is no act or legislation which allows you to charge this fee. You can charge what you want. However there is an obligation to inform the patient of what you are charging. This will be difficult to defend. My advice would be to write off this amount and talk to the patient and try and smooth the waters.

entries.question: I need your assistance, there are quite a few practices that lately asked to charge an admin fee/levy. There are no BHF tariff code provided for the dr's to charge it Discovery provided us with a code "00000" that the practices can use.
entries.answer: When the RVUs were calculated for the tariff codes one of the ‘building blocks’ was the associated administration cost. This means that the administrative cost is already included in the fee and adding an additional code is effectively charging twice for the same thing. The RSSA will not develop or support a separate administrative fee. My information is that Discovery does not support the use of 00000 for an administrative fee. The RSSA position is that 00000 can be used to indicate that there is no code or the coding field can be left blank. Should a practice consider the benefits to be inadequate the option of charging private rates is available.

entries.question: Could you look into this advert on Facebook ? https://www.facebook.com/HermanusOncology/videos/911090589099458/ I was under the impression that medical professionals are not allowed to advertise ? He specifically says that it is not necessary to travel to Cape Town and “ over the mountain” anymore. Your opinion ?
entries.answer: This is a difficult area as HPCSA has withdrawn its document on advertising and replaced it with the Business Practice document. In general terms practitioners are allowed to notify doctors and the public of their services, where they are what they do etc. They are not allowed to say that they are more qualified (other than being a specialist) or that their services are better than anyone else’s The 'over the mountain’ statement is a comment, albeit clumsy, about where they are. I don’t think that if a complaint was made to HPCSA on this that it would be deemed as a problem. As previously mentioned the HPCSA document on advertising was withdrawn and replaces with booklet 11: 'Guidelines on Over-servicing, Perverse Incentives and Related Matters.’, which I have attached. Advertising is covered in its definition: 2 DEFINING OF CONCEPTS For the purpose of these guidelines, the following concepts will have the meanings described below, unless the context indicates otherwise. It should be noted that these concepts have not been defined for legal purposes, but merely to clarify the meaning of the guidelines. It should be noted that some of these definitions have been based on those in certain Acts of Parliament in the Medicine and Substance Related Act, 1965 (Act No. 101 of 1965 2.1 “Advertise” in relation to any health establishment or orthodox medicine, complementary medicine, veterinary medicine, medical device or scheduled substance or health related product or health related service, means any written, pictorial, visual or other descriptive matter or verbal statement or reference in respect thereof: 2.1.1 Appearing in any newspaper, magazine, pamphlet or other publication; or 2.1.2 Distributed to members of the public; or 2.1.3 Brought to the notice of members of the public in any manner whatsoever, That is intended to promote the sale of that orthodox medicine, complementary medicine, veterinary medicine, medical device or scheduled substance or health related product or to attract patients to any particular health establishment or health related service Reading this and especially 2.1.3, the Facebook video falls under the definition of advertising. The question is whether it is allowable. In 'Advertising Rule 3' we see the following: Healthcare practitioners shall not advertise or endorse or encourage the use of any health establishment or orthodox medicine, complementary medicine, veterinary medicine, medical device or scheduled substance or health related product or health- related service in a manner that unfairly promotes the practice of a particular healthcare practitioner or a healthcare facility for the purpose of financial gain or other valuable consideration. The emphasis here is on the word ‘unfairly’. My view again is, by simply making patients and doctors aware that a facility is available would not be construed as ‘unfair’

entries.question: We are in discussion with the neurologists at our facility with regard to correct protocols for “After-Hour” scanning of stroke patients. In patients with suspicion of stroke, where thrombolysis is being considered, would it still be correct to do an initial CT scan of the brain, followed by MRI, if no bleed is seen? Or is the current protocol one of going directly to MRI scanning without the initial CT? Please could you advise regarding the Radiology Society’s thinking on this.
entries.answer: RSSA was asked a similar question in 2016. Our consensus recommendations at that time were: 1. Acute stroke up to 12 hours in recognised stroke unit (i.e. where there are facilities for acute intervention): 1. NECT Brain: if haemorrhage nothing further, if no haemorrhage: 2. NECT Brain ; CT Angio Carotids and COW + contrast brain. +- CT perfusion 2. ? Non-acute stroke, TIA, ‘wake up stroke’ 1. Flair, DWI ADT and T2* and TOF of COW. (10450) If positive doppler US of extra cranial vessels indicated as first line of investigation. No indication for routine TOF or contrast exam of extra cranial vessels.

entries.question: We are in the process of discussing whether we should go into an agreement with the hospital with regards to renting their MRI compatible anaesthetic machine on a monthly basis. For selected MRI and CT patients there is the need for using the anaesthetic equipment sometimes, but we are unsure whether there is any code linked to cover for our outlay of this. Would you perhaps be able to assist us in this regard? How can we get to charge this on the patients’ medical aid? The anaesthetists will be using the equipment on our premises and obviously they will charge their fees as well.
entries.answer: There is no radiology code for use of the MRI compatible anaesthetic machine. 2. Common practice by radiologists who have owned and used such machines over the years, is that the cost of using the machine is not recoverable from the medical aid nor the patients, and is an unrecoverable practice cost. 3. In the event that your practice does not own such equipment, and rents it from the hospital, the terms of any commercial agreement will be concluded directly between you and the hospital. 4. Code 02010 - ECG/pulse Oximeter monitoring – may well be charged if owned and used by the practice during the procedure.

entries.question: Can you please indicate if the same principle apply for angiography that is included in the interventional procedure tariff code? The practice is giving me a hard time about billing this in combination.
entries.answer: A a general rule the interventional procedure includes the diagnostic angiogram done at the same sitting which is necessary to road map the interventional procedure. If an angiogram is done purely to make the diagnosis and or to assess the feasibility of intervention and is done at a separate sitting that is allowable. Often this is replaced by a CT or MR angio. There should be some flexibility in this approach but the intention is that the diagnostic angiogram should not be repeated at the time of intervention if the indications for intervention are clear and a diagnosis has been made.

entries.question: advise whether I can form an association between me and a radiographer and be able to bill at radiology rates for general radiology. The assessment and report will be done by me.
entries.answer: The appropriate regulations are copied below: From this it is clear that you can both form a group practice with or employ a radiographer. The issue of billing becomes a little more complex. It is clearly allowable for you to employ a radiographer and report on all exams and charge specialist radiology rates. This is the basis of current private practice. However billing in group practices becomes a little more complicated. The only reference we can find relates to a specialist and GP practicing together: Partnerships and juristic persons.—(1) Where a patient is seen— (a) by both a medical specialist or a dental specialist and a medical practitioner or a dentist practising as specified in rule 8 (3), such specialist and medical practitioner or dentist shall charge the fees applicable to either the medical practitioner or the dentist and not those applicable to a medical specialist or a dental specialist; and (b) by a medical specialist or a dental specialist only, the fees applicable to such specialist may be charged. The implies that in a multidisciplinary group practice if both the GP and specialist see the patient GP rates are applicable and if just the specialist specialist rates. The subtlety here is that both provide the same consultative service. However in a radiology practice the radiologist and radiographer provide different parts of the service and as long as the radiologist provides a report on each examination done by the radiographer, specialist radiologist rates may be charged in exactly the same way as if the radiologist employed the radiographer. The profit share between the radiologist /radiographer is a commercial negotiation. You could achieve the same thing by an employment contract with the radiographer with a formula for radiographer remuneration related to workload. Profit sharing is permissible as both are registered persons and both take a commensurate part in the procedure. In summary: you can form a group practice with a radiographer and can charge specialist radiologist rates as long as you report on each study.

entries.question: What do you recommend to use for colonic transit study where AXR is done as control view, one AXR view done when tablet is swallowed, another AXR on day 3 and then again on day 5. Total of 4 AXR images. Should we bill it once off, or over 3 days = 3 visits to complete the requested procedure
entries.answer: One AXR fee per day is appropriate.

entries.question: Billing Combination
entries.answer: We have been approached by Verirad to comment on the billing combination of plain film lumbar spines and pelvis. i.e any account which has one of the spine codes and the code for pelvis. i.e (53100, 53110, 53120, 53130) and (55100 or 56120) on the same account. There is no indication for the routine addition of pelvis to a lumbar spine examination for acute or chronic backache. The pelvis may be added when there are clinical indications and requested by the referring doctor or at the discretion of the radiologist with specific indications. Verirad figures reflect that the variation in the use of this combination of codes, using 2015 figures, is from 7% to 95% with an average of 30%. These figures indicate a need to be vigilant about the possibility that there may be inappropriate usage. We accordingly recommend that you assess where your practice sits relative to the 30% combination, which seems to offer a reasonable benchmark. Practices should be able to determine their own percentages and make adjustments if appropriate.

entries.question: The guidelines for requesting X-ray examinations from the Department of Health, Directorate of Radiation Control refer. These guidelines are dated October 2009 and are attached. A literal reading of these guidelines would seem to imply that the requests have to be signed by the person requesting the examination (namely the referring doctor or specialist as in point number 1). Paragraph 3.3 does not provide a practical alternative. The nursing staff referred to in 3.3 include only primary health care nurses or occupational safety nurses. Apparently some Medical Aids have objected to payments for radiological services since the original request form was not signed by the referring doctor. To date this has been a rare occurrence, but we feel that the regulation as it stands could be used against radiologists by Medical Aids in a truly aggravating way if it not altered. We therefore request clarification in this regard. It would seem impractical to insist on a signature from the referreing doctor on every occasion. There are certainly times when the clinicians communicate with nursing staff and ICU staff telephonically and request them to attain some further radiological results in order to enable further clinical decision-making. It would be impossible for the clinician to be present on each occasion to sign off a request. To give just one example, a surgeon who is operating may give instructions to the wards between cases – it would be impossible for him or her to sign each request in person without causing excessive delay in all such cases. The same would apply in many real – world clinical situations. We therefore request comment from our esteemed colleagues of the Radiological Society on this matter and we trust that medical Aids will not be able to turn to this clause to avoid paying for radiological procedures.
entries.answer: These regulations have been in place for some time and we have not had any significant problems with or complaints about them to date. They are designed for radiographers and are good practice when ionising radiation is involved. The concept of a signature is outdated with on line referral, but all imaging examinations should have a referral note, either analogue or digital as a record of referral to protect the referring doctor, radiographer and radiologist. Digital referrals will identify the referrer and be time date stamped. Para 3.3 is prefixed by an ‘e.g’ as examples of nursing staff, the examples are not exclusive. Medical Schemes may stipulate that referral is necessary to provide benefits. There appears to be an industry wide agreement that referral letters are not required for screening examinations such as mammography. I hope this helps. Please continue the conversation if you need more info.

entries.question: For requested paediatric chest and abdomen done on 1 image, we are being asked to fee only chest or abdomen but not both. However both regions are reported. Are we allowed to fee two regions if a baby gram is done?
entries.answer: Difficult, if either chest or abdomen charged radiologist time underecovered and radiographers over. Usual practice is to charge the higher of the two if both are done on a single view and both reported.

entries.question: Please can you assist us with the correct tariff code for billing an: ARTERIAL DOPPLER OF THE TEMPORAL ARTERIES (ADULT)
entries.answer: There is no code for this procedure. The temporal artery is extracranial so best equivalent is 20220 - Ultrasound of the carotid arteries, bilateral including B mode, pulsed and colour doppler

entries.question: We have a problem with Verrirad. Our billing and payment is rejected for example they state that only AP and lateral views are required for lumbar vertebrae and they state that this is according to RSSA protocol and guidelines. Also the question of pre-contrast CT scans and numerous other examples. Could you please supply us with with these guidelines and protocols.
entries.answer: The RSSA interacts with Verirad and other funders almost on a daily basis. Our position is that we do not want to assess individual accounts, that is the job of the scheme's medical advisors, but advise on principles of appropriateness and review practice profiles. Practice profiles were used by Discovery to assess billing efficiency and form the basis of the RSSA/Discovery Peer Review programme in 2015. Discovery has decided that the annual assessment of ‘efficiency’ has served its purpose and will be moving more to quality metrics. One of the main problems picked up in the peer review process was upcoding. Radiation exposure should always be at the absolute minimum. In addition radiology utilization is increasing to such an extent that we are likely to see more schemes offering sub-inflation annual increases to keep their costs down. The existing tariff, although imperfect, has different levels of examination for different indications. The procedure used should always be the least complex which provides a safe diagnostic margin in the majority of cases. If Verirad is pushing you to downcode it suggests that your profile is different from your peers. Many large practices are using a two view lumbar spine for the assessment of non traumatic back ache, sponyloslysis almost invariably can be picked up on the lateral view without the need for obliques. If additional view are needed they can be requested. Our advice to Verirad is that in non-traumatic backache, without red flags, a two view examination is the standard, four views can be done when indicated in a small percentage of cases. If you can find me any recent literature showing that standard series of the lumbar spine for non-traumatic backache should be 4 views please send it to me? Going even further you asked about guidelines. RSSA endorses IRefer. I have attached their guidelines for imaging back ache. There are virtually no indications for plain film examination of the lumbar spine except suspected osteoporotic collapse in the elderly. Pain with ‘red flags’ gets MRI, the rest, apart from the suspected osteoporotic collapse get nothing. The other example you give is pre-contrast CT scans. The RSSA position is that pre and post contrast CT is rarely needed with modern equipment. If you have recent evidence from the literature which indicates that pre and post contrast CT is required as routine or more often than not please send it to me? Lumbar spine and CT examinations will be further bundled in the new coding structure to take away the choice and possibility of up coding. If you think that Verirad is applying these recommendations unreasonably, pleas send me specific examples.

entries.question: How do we fee for this scenario: Say for example a doctor refers a patient to us for an x-ray of the left elbow, and we do comparative views of the right elbow – are we allowed to charge for both elbows? Or only if the referring doctors requests that we x-ray both elbows? Generally, we do comparative views for all children under the age of 18 years.
entries.answer: There are no indications for doing comparative views in children for limb trauma either referred or non-referred. A radiologist is expected to be sufficiently trained and skilled to assess the growth plates in children without the need for comparison views. The billing question, therefore, does not need an answer. I have attached the relevant page from iRefer which are the RSSA endorsed RCR guidelines for clinical referral to radiology.

entries.question: Please can you advise on the following scenarios, as we get co-operation from most practices on this, but others not. Can we enforce it, or, if the practice is not happy to make amendments, do we have to accept? 1) 10410 and 51410. (Mr brain and MR cervical and cranio-cervical junction) Most practices oblige when we request down code to 51400 since done in combination with 10410, but others don’t and feel that since they need to change the coil that the full fee should be billed. Please comment on the following as well where again we get some practices (more than not) that bill the 2nd region as a limited study (53400), and others not. 2) 55400 and 53410 (MR bony pelvis and MR lumbar spine) 56410 and 53410 (MR of the hip joints and MR lumbar spine) 51410 and 53410 (MR cervical and cranio-cervical junction and MR lumbar spine) 52410 and 53410 (MR T spine and MR L spine) 51410, 52410 and 53410 (MR C spine, MR T spine and MR L spine : When all 3 billed, should 1 not be billed as a full study and the other 2 as limited?
entries.answer: The RSSA advice of 2010 still applies; 'The billing of contiguous region MR studies has long been contentious. This was a discussion point at the recent meeting with Verirad. Most practices handle these studies as follows: (1) Lumbar and scout of thoracic spine billed as full + limited (most commonly requested) (2) Brain and scout cervical billed as full brain and limited cervical study. If for some reason complete studies of two contiguous regions (with all sag, coronal and axial views) are done then I suppose one could justify two full fees. (Some practice will often do 2nd region at some reduced rate, depending on the circumstances. The need for this type of studies in our experience however is not common.' How it is controlled is up to you. If you see practices whose profiles differ markedly from the norm you should interrogate them and make your own funding decisions. Guidelines should not be used to disallow funding in specific cases but should be used as a benchmark to assess profiles.

entries.question: RSSA and Discovery guidelines for breast MRI. Please forward these to me at your earliest convenience.
entries.answer: Discovery will fund a symptomatic breast MRI in the same way as any other MRI. Screening breast MRI follows their breast screening guidelines and will require the patient to have gone through their classification system on the Discovery website and be deemed high risk and MR appropriate.

entries.question: What are the guidelines in terms of billing for Radiologists call outs when reporting emergency cases from home? In our practice we only report emergency cases after hours but with home reporting the Radioligist doesn’t necessarily go to the branches for every case anymore. There are medical aids who are now querying whether the report was done “via teleradiology” (as they still call it) or whether the doctor was on site. Surely it doesn’t matter whether he had to drive to a branch to report or report from his own study as he was still called to do a report, right? How would subsequent call outs be treated? In the past if a radiologist was at the branch already, we charged a call out for the 1st case and subsequent call outs for the cases thereafter.
entries.answer: The codes were devised for radiologists actually travelling. The reduction is as the radiologist is already there. From home the radiologist does not have to travel and so this code was used as a compromise. We will look at the wording in the new version. The use of this code is slightly controversial as it could be argued that it is part of the after hours increase we received a few years ago. The challenge to radiology will be the use of both AI and offshore reporting for after hours work. Our challenge is to ensure that we compete by providing 24 hours reporting.

entries.question: I would like to know the RSSA's ( and medical aids) official policy on casualty doctors ordering advanced imaging without referring patients to relevant specialists. In our particular case we have a mix of casualty officers, some of which have run our casualty for many years, and do sessions various step down hospitals in essentially a medical officer role, and others who are fresh out of community service. One or two of the older ones have an interest in musculo-skeletal pathology and perhaps have done a course or diploma on such and have labelled themselves "musculo-skeletal physicians". As we do not have a trauma surgeon in the hospital we allow the casualty officers to order acute trauma investigations such as CT Brain and C-Spines and trauma chest and abdomens, although the patient will get admitted under the name of one of our general surgeons. We have not had any problems form the medical aids with this approach thus far and the purpose is not to prevent the patient form having emergent imaging in a timeous fashion and also trusting to a certain extent, the clinical acumen of the casualty officers to order appropriate investigations. The problem comes in with MRI, where due to expense, as well as appropriateness of imaging we have insisted on the patient at least being discussed with a specialist. We have tried to insist that a specialist name be on the request form and we have washed our hands of whether the relevant specialist sees the patient. Some specialists have allowed the casualty officers to use their name when requesting MRI's and we don't know whether they are informed of any of the clinical details of the patient before the casualty officers send the request. It is impossible to call the specialist every time to ask if they have seen the patient and we gave up at the point of warning the relevant specialist that the medical will eventually red flag them as high users of imaging because they are allowing junior staff to order special investigations in their name. We have left that to their own conscience. I have also warned the head of casualty of the morbidity created by incidental findings on unnecessary scans resulting in serious morbidity for patients, as in the case of unnecessary MRI for lower back pain. It has been an ongoing problem especially as the head of casualty is the chairman of the MAC meetings and they are of the impression that we are questioning their clinical acumen and also being obstructive. In my opinion we are being pragmatic when it comes to trauma imaging in allowing them to order urgent CT's and they wouldn't be able to do even that were there a trauma surgeon on the premises. With regard to MRI's,i don't think the medical aids would care what msk diploma a casualty officer may have, imaging would have to be requested by a specialist. Please could you send your response or the guidelines on an official letterhead as I was asked to provide proof of my assertions(I feel like i'm being asked to reinvent the wheel). Please also confirm that our practice was audited by Discovery(when we had that Skype conference call with you) for high usage which we could justify at the time due to our hospitals employment of new specialists etc, as I informed them that I would not be able to justify accepting requests for special investigations from casualty officers based on one or two of them having special interests in certain things. I added that we do not accept requests for CT's and MRI's from GP's regardless of how many years they have been practicing. The point is to limit unnecessary investigations.
entries.answer: There are a couple of general principles here. The first is authorisation of payments according to medical scheme rules. A specilaist's name should not appear on a referral letter to facilitate medical schemes payment unless the specialist has been involved in the specific case. Using a specialist's name without his or her knowledge, in each case, to facilitate payment would be fraudulent both by the radiologist and the specialist involved. This is irrespective of the type of examination. The reason that you may not have had trouble from the medical aids is that they don’t know about it? It is important for data collection that referrals are correctly allocated. The private hospitals calculate a 'cost per event’ for each admission and do not like ‘expensive’ specialists. Therefore, apart from the ethical consideration, it is not in the interests of specialists to have referrals allocated to them for patients they are not involved in. Discovery Health will add the names of GP’s who work in casualty departments to their list of authorised referrers for casualty CT. Please contact Elrina Ferreira about this: ElrinaF@discovery.co.za We are finding out whether or not the other major administrators have the same policy and will update you once we have this information. Secondly, while the above applies to MRI, there appears to be an appropriateness issue with MRI referral. There are no indications for MRI, in the absence of trauma and 'red flags’ for uncomplicated backache. MRI referral is usually linked to specialist to ensure appropriateness . If the casualty officers are referring you inappropriate MRI, it is correct that you question the appropriateness of the referral and their clinical acumen. The way things are going is that we expect, in future, with the introduction of Clinical Decision Support (CDS) that the ability to refer will be linked to the appropriateness of the request , not the qualifications of the referrer, conversely the qualifications of a referrer do not guarantee appropriate referral. Please let me know if this sorts out your problem and whether you still need a document you can pass on to your referrers.

entries.question: Is there anything in place like a “certificate of need” which would prevent someone opening a practice within a certain radius from an existing practice? Would there be a difference in this between radiology and Nuclear Medicine (ie scarce skill)? Is there a difference between provinces? I received a question from dr Maharaj in KZN - another practice put in a gamma camera within a km or 2 from her, which now obviously impacts on her income...
entries.answer: Private practice wanting legislative protection is always interesting!. The basis of a free market is that it s not regulated. The National Health Act has provision for Certificate of Need (CON) but the proposed regulations were withdrawn in 2014. There is effectively no national CON in place. The Western Cape has its own regulations which act as a CON, but these are unlikely to withstand legal challenge. KZN produced a discussion document in 2002 but, to my knowledge has never implemented regulations. So the answer is no, there is no provincial or national CON at this stage.

entries.question: Could you possibly assist? I have been asked to look into this but have been unable to find any applicable information. We have had to employ an “Authorizations Clerk” who is employed to authorize all CT and MRI scans. Is there any tariff code available to Radiologists to charge for the service whereby we are obtaining authorization on behalf of the patient?
entries.answer: There is no code for this. Part of global practice expense.

entries.question: Please can you advise on the billing of contiguous regions in paediatric patients. Example 1 : Hand and wrist requested on a 2 year old. Whilst imaging of both are done together, they are being billed separately Example 2 : If a knee, tib/fib, ankle is requested on a 3 month old, can each region be billed separately? Billing as per above is unique to only a couple of practices nationwide, billing 2 or 3 regions on a child, when all can be done on 1 film, and then comparison view sometimes also billed. Since the billing of a technical and a professional component is not catered for in the fee structure, please advise as to appropriate billing for these scenarios.
entries.answer: There are two components to this, the time taken to position and process the study and the professional component in reporting it. The radiologist is overcharging for the first if two regions are done on one exposure but being under remunerated for the professional component for the second if done in one exposure. As always it is about balance. We would recommend that a single charge is billed for contiguous regions when included in a single study.

entries.question: Could you kindly advice on the most appropriate code for Contrast Enhanced Digital Mammography (CEDM). This test is done at the Steve Biko Academic Hospital.
entries.answer: There is no code, suggest standard mammography code plus contrast and disposables

entries.question: Please could you advise the RSSA as to the status of any doctor who wishes to attend an overseas congress? Are they able to claim CPD points for the specific congress attended? Wil the HPCSA acknowledge these congresses? If an overseas congress is acceptable to the HPCSA in order to obtain CPD points, is there any particular arrangement that one needs to make prior to their attendance? Please also advise on the requirements should doctors complete any questionaires in overseas journals.
entries.answer: Conferences, symposia, refresher courses, short courses without a measurable outcome, international conferences (must be approved by a South African Accreditor if not accredited/ recognized for CEU equivalent in the country where it was held).

entries.question: Aangeheg 2 eise met verwysings en verslag. Dr de Beer het nog nie motivering geskryf . Hy het gevra ons eers uitsluitsel kry by RSSA ivm die skrywe ontvang van CAMAF aangaande die reël wat bestaan oor die gebruik van 100ml kontras , waarvan hy nie bewus is nie. Volgens ons Radioloë gebruik hul 100ml vir volwasse pasiënte en 50ml of minder vir kinders.
entries.answer: If a CT scan is done for a clinical suspicion of a brain tumour or metastases, best practice, accepted by Ann Osborne, is that 100 ml. is the appropriate contrast dose. If CT scan is done after hours for trauma there is usually no indication for the use of intravenous contrast if the pre contract scan is normal. If there is any abnormality on the pre-contrast scan 100 of contrast would be accepted as an appropriate dose.

entries.question: Could I please get your advise on what I need to do viz advising a patient of mine who inadvertantly got a CT abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast done while she is pregnant. She didn't know or think she pregnant. She said she had had a miscarriage in Sept of this year. Gestation only discovered at CT. Ultrasound gestational age = 13 weeks. Live foetus. DLP = 1192.98
entries.answer: There is quite a good overview here: https://www.ajronline.org/doi/full/10.2214/AJR.09.3723 Slightly increased risk of childhood cancer. Patient should be advised of this and if necessary referred to a geneticist.

entries.question: Please can you assist with ongoing queries that we receive with regard to CT IVP’s and after hours fee billing. 1) CT IVP Please can you advise if there has been a change in protocol for CT IVP for ? renal stones. Despite the directive received (“Appropriate billing” letter dated Nov 2011 – extract below), we are seeing more and more practices performing 40337 (CT Abdomen pre and post) as their standard protocol and some even insisting on 40340 (triphasic) at the request for a CT IVP for ? renal stones. Their argument being they require delayed films as per an IVP, or that the referring Urologist requires this protocol. <image003.png> They are requesting 40337 at Pre-auth level, and regardless of findings on uncontrasted scan, they continue with the contrasted examination and feel entitlement to pay since this is their standard protocol, and they managed to get this tariff authorised. Either that, or, since these examinations are often done after hours auth only requested post examination, how does the scheme authorise when they have already perfomed and charged either 40337 or 40340 despite the uncontrasted findings and a 42300 would have been appropriate, but then want to be reimbursed for the examination they performed? One response we received back when we suggested 42300 as the appropriate tariff, as follows: Your attention is drawn to the request card where the patient is noted to present with right flank pain. Authorisation was obtained for pre and post contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis as per our protocol. The requesting doctors have indicated that in the absence of a stone demonstrated on the CT renal tract stone protocol study, we are to proceed with a post contrast series through the abdomen and pelvis usually including a delayed phase. This is the reason for us performing a biphasic study in this patient. In addition, the study was performed as per the authorisation obtained. Please advise if you are in agreement with the following when a patient is forwarded for CT IVP for ? renal calc: 1) If a patient presents with haematuria and no renal calculus found on un contrasted examination then a contrasted exam should follow to find the source of haematuria. 2) If a patient presents with haematuria and renal calc/s are found, then no need to continue with the contrasted study. 3) IF the Practice insists on contrast (they normal bill 150mls ) or the referring Practitioner states on referral note: “CT IVP with contrast”, but stones identifed on the uncontrasted scan, yet they continue with contrast as per request. In this instance (and as per attached account) do we: - Auth/pay 42300 and reject the contrast since not required - Auth/pay 42300 and the cost of the contrast - Auth/pay just 40333 (post contrast scan) since they have already used contrast - Auth/pay 40337 (pre and post contrast scan) Please see attached account, referral and report and advise of appropriate tariff code as the Practice is not in agreement that 42300 would have been the appropriate tariff code. 2) Charging of tariffs 01100, 01200, 01300, 01400, 01500 and 01600 We continally get queries regarding the above tariffs, and whether or not they can be billed at all since, as per last available guidleines available, they have no fee attached. Most schemes reject these as Scheme exclusions. Dr Orman advised us year ago that he has discussed this tariff with RSSA in the past, who have advised that the member is liable only if the Radiologist informed the member of the intended charge. Is this correct? Please advise on the following: 1) Can this tariff be billed, and if so at what rate? 2) If this tariff is billed, and it is a scheme exclusion, then is the member liable regardless whether or not they were advised of this fee prior to the examinations being performed.
entries.answer: RENAL STONES etc. There are two main areas here which overlap. If the primary concern is renal calculi, low dose, non contrast CT is indicated. If the clinical problem is painless macroscopic haematuria CT IVU is indicated. It is not always easy to allocate to one of these categories, but usually it will be clear cut. CT IVU is usually a single phase and only needs pre contrast if that has not been done before. We cannot support pre and post contrast studies as routine in every case of suspected renal colic. I agree that there is no indication for post contrast in the example you have sent. We have simplified the coding system in our current coding revision but cannot publish these at present due to Competition Commission constraints. Our guidelines are available in iRefer, the RSSA endorsed RCR guidelines. There are available at: Iphone: https://itunes.apple.com/za/app/rcr-irefer/id592373201?mt=8 Ipad: https://itunes.apple.com/za/app/rcr-irefer-for-ipad/id771719962?mt=8 Android Phone: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=air.uk.org.mbur As in previous correspondence we cannot advise what you pay for in each specific case. That is your call with input from your radiology advisor. AFTER HOURS Detailed comment was sent to our members in August 2013. The summary was: A After hours procedure codes: Members should not use the codes 01100, 01200, 10300, 01400, 01500 and 01600 for either in or out patient visits by medical aids After hours call codes: Members use the After Hours Call Fees, 01010 and 01020 appropriately as per our recommendation of March 2013. These are only recommendations, each radiologist has the choice whether or not they are used and at what rate they are billed. Every practitioner has the obligation of informing patients if codes and tariffs are not covered by the member’s medical aid and is at risk of attack by the HPCSA if he or she fails to do that. We will be issuing a detailed medical legal recommendation on this is in the near future.

entries.question: I just want to confirm if we can add the after hours tariff codes for medical aid patients? PROCEDURE AFTER HOURS 01100 ULTRASOUND PROCEDURE A/H PP 01200 CT PROCEDURE A/H PER PROC 01300 MR PROCEDURE A/H PER PROCEDURE 01400 ANGIOGRAPHY PROCED A/H PP 01500 INTERVENTIONAL PROCED A/H PP 01600 We did a test run and all the medical aids rejected the tariffs.
entries.answer: You are correct. The medical aids will reject these codes. Please see the attached afterhours notice sent to RSSA members during Aug 2013. Extract from the notice below - AFTER HOURS PROCEDURE CODES • 01100 X-ray procedure after hours, per procedure • 01200 Ultrasound procedure after hours, per procedure • 01300 CT procedure after hours, per procedure • 01400 MR procedure after hours, per procedure • 01500 Angiography procedure after hours, per procedure • 01600 Interventional procedure after hours, per procedure It is the use of these codes which is causing concern and to which the recommended changes apply. These are not paid by the schemes.

entries.question: With CT scans the patients often drink their contrast from the night before the scan, in this case we use 1 – 2 bottles of contrast as there is sufficient time for the contrast to travel through. This, however, is not always possible if the referring doctor requires the CT the same day (which happens often), on these occasions we need to use 3 bottles of contrast as 2 is not sufficient. If we don’t give enough contrast we end up having to give the patient an enema. If we use 3 bottles and charge the patient / medical aid for what was used is this acceptable or does the practice have to carry this cost? The reason I ask is because we have received Verirad queries, according to them the nationwide billing trend for this consumable varies between 1 – 2 bottles and are requesting for motivation if 3 are used. What is the RSSA’s view on this?
entries.answer: It appears that two bottles in the norm, however if you occasionally need three bottles and it is not routine in your practice, Verirad should accept this.

entries.question: CT SINUSES
entries.answer: You don’t mention it but I trust that you have seen the recent update we sent to members, copied below. The addendum purely indicated which codes should be used for cone beam studies. We had to send this out due to persistent upcoding and tariff abuse. The answer to your question is that it is up to you, we made the recommendation that full sinuses should only be done on referral by an ENT surgeon. Conversely there are ENT surgeons who only ask for limited sinuses. I don’t think that every patient referred by a specialist should have full sinus as the vast majority do not go to surgery, yet we want to avoid re-radaition especially in children. The solution is in our next coding update, which we due to competition commission constraints cannot publish, thee we have a singe code for CT sinuses with a weighted average fee. Suggest you bill as you think appropriate and let me know if you have any problems. we will support you if you occasionally do full sinuses for specialists other than ENT surgeons but will find it difficult to do so if you do them for all.

entries.question: Is it possible to register a radiology practice as an incorporated company with the executor of the deceased estate as one of the members if the executor is not a qualified radiologist. And it possible to get a practice number with the above mentioned conditions ?
entries.answer: 1. Only natural persons registered with the HPCSA may be shareholders or directors of an incorporate company. 2. Only a person who is a practitioner may be a director.3. Following the death of a shareholder the estate may hold shares for 6 months or longer with approval of the HPCSA. 4. In the event of death of a shareholder as in 3 above, the shareholder shall not allow the estate to act as a director or receive, directly or indirectly any share of income or profit earned by the company.

entries.question: Do you know whose responsibility it is to supply the hospital’s theatre staff and surgeons with dosimeters? Does it matter who the C-Arm belongs to?
entries.answer: Whoever holds the license for the device producing the radiation.

entries.question: Charge for foreign citizens and foreign insurance Please let me know whether we are allowed to charge foreign rates? A foreign citizen was admitted to our Hospital and we understand the Hospital is charging foreign rates. What is the opinion of the RSSA? Do you have foreign rates guidelines?
entries.answer: Please advise that a practitioner is allowed to determine his or her own fees. There are no statutory fees. Benefits are determined locally by medical schemes. RSSA cannot advise on fee rates. So a practitioner can decide what rate to charge foreign visitors. The decision to be made is there a justification in charging different rates for the same service if payment is guaranteed?

entries.question: Our Radiologists have advised us that they cannot do gestation scans as there is an extra indemnity amount that they have to pay to cover them. Is this only for scans done after 12 weeks?Please could I have clarification on this
entries.answer: Normal cover only extends to scans done up to 13 weeks.

entries.question: We have become aware of a GP with an x-ray unit licensed under a radiographer’s name. He is doing Industrial CXR and apparently also reporting on these. According to Rad Control, the unit is legit as it is registered under a radiation worker which we know is legal but they also said that he is suppose to use a radiologist for reporting. Is there a way that RSSA can enquire whether he is in actual fact reporting the cases himself and put a stop to it or what is your stance on this one?
entries.answer: We have had discussions around this one over the years. Our position has always been that radiologists should report on occupational health chests but we have no way of enforcing this. The Department of Radiation Protection will not licence GPs but will licence a radiation worker. GPs can report on the exams. Unfortunately nothing we can do about this. HPCSA will simply respond that as long as it is on their own patients it is OK.

entries.question: We receive request every now and then from GP doctors requesting for scans. Will you be able to assist us with any documents stating that GP’s are not allowed to sign referral letters for specialised radiology. We want to forward this letter to referring GP’s.
entries.answer: There are no HPCSA or RSSA regulations defining who may refer what. Technically any registered medical practitioner may refer a patient to a radiologist for any study. The issue is that the individual medical scheme rules dictate which referrals are funded. Most schemes will not pay for CT and MRI referral from GPs. Making it more complicated the rules are not generic or uniform and some schemes allow GP referral for high tech for GPS enrolled in networks (e.g Discovery Keycare)

entries.question: Medical aids are rejecting code I70.9 as being invalid. Our doctors told us to replace it with I70.8, They are still rejecting as invalid. The partners are at a bit of a loss here Do you have any ideas what we can use?
entries.answer: The ICD10 codes marked in red were discontinued effective 1 July 2013

entries.question: Verirad requested the referral note and report for X-rays done on patient Prudence Royle (copies attached) They now asked us to forward the images and our radiologists would like RSSA opinion on this.
entries.answer: We have had similar requests before. The reason is to confirm that the sections billed have actually been done. The RSSA position is that, as long as there is patient permission for release of data, that members should cooperate in these type of requests, as long as they are reasonable and occasional. The problem here is that three adjacent segments have been charged for, forearm, hand and wrist. Although these three areas have been requested, common practice would be to charge for one or two sections rather than three, as a fall onto an outstretched hand is usually a wrist injury. The enquiry is to ensure that three complete studies have been performed and not combined on to a reduced number of images

entries.question: Wanneer ons dokters “Home Affairs” se “Radiology Report” teken vra ons die pasiente se oorspronklike paspoort. Ons kry pasiente wat nie hulle paspoorte bring nie en dan erg te kere gaan en aandring dat hulle gehelp word sonder hulle paspoorte. Is daar iewers ‘n Wet of Reël wat ons vir hulle kan wys dat dit volgens Wet so werk.
entries.answer: I cannot find any documentation which specifies the identification process for Home Affairs. A radiologist would be at risk if he certified a patient free of tuberculosis without positive identification and confident knowledge that the patient x-rayed was the same one as on the certificate. Recommendation: A patient should be identified by means of a recognised photo ID, preferably a passport or an identity document. A copy of the document used for identification and a copy of the completed certificate should be retained by the radiologist for a period of six years.

entries.question: As a followup of my telephonic conversation with you regarding DR VV Moodley query: Who’s respeonsibility is it to order the U& E Blood tests where the Radiologist is administering contrast. Who’s reponsibility is to study the test results and determine whether the contrasted study is to be performed depending on the test results.
entries.answer: 1. The answer to the first question is that it is by local arrangement. The radiologist, in certain circumstances , needs to see these results. If these tests have been ordered by another party the radiologists does not need to order the blood tests but if they have not been ordered and they are necessary the radiologist must do it. 2. The radiologist is responsible for prescribing and supervising the injection of contrast. It is therefore the radiologist's responsibility to ensure that it is safe to do so. This includes reviewing the blood tests in appropriate circumstances.

entries.question: There are a number of residents finished with their exams but NOT research or resident time. Are they legally allowed to do locums in private practice? They are also not on the HPCSA iRegister yet.
entries.answer: Registrars who have completed their exams but have not commuted the research requirements for the MMed, may in some circumstances be given permission to work in the public sector. They should not be employed in the private sector unless registered as a specialist radiologist for independence practice. If there was a problem, the radiologist and the practice could both be held liable and the indemnity insurer could refuse cover. If, however, in the absence of registration they have a letter from the HPCSA confirming that they are able to practice as a specialist radiologist in independent practice this would be permissible. The latter scenario is highly unlikely.

entries.question: I would like to know at what age is a patient allowed to start having a Mammogram? We are experiencing some issues with a certain medial aid regarding their patients being able to have a Mammogram before the age of 50. Your assistance in this matter will be greatly appreciated
entries.answer: The RSSA recommends that all woman, with or without increased risk factors enter a screening mammography program at the age of 40 and have mammography performed annually from that age. In the case of a family history of breast cancer and a close family member, mother or sister having been diagnosed with breast cancer at an early age, it is recommended that mammography should start five years earlier than the age at which the family member was diagnosed with breast cancer. For example: if a patient???s mother was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 40 the patient should start mammography at age 35. The RSSA follows international recommendations that high-risk patients, those with a strong family history or are BRCA gene positive should have annual screening MRI, in conjunction with mammography from the age of 40, or earlier as in the case of mammography when a family member has been diagnosed with breast cancer at an early age.

entries.question: She is wanting to know if a patient is 40+ do they need a referral letter from a GP for a mammogram or can they just book an appointment directly themselves. The reason she asks is that the practice she is at now insists on a referral letter (previous practice did not). Dr Harvey – GP she is in communication with states that referral letter is not necessary if patient is 40+.
entries.answer: The comment below applies, irrespective of age. For a screening procedure a referral letter is not necessary unless the patient’s medical aid needs one. Every patient screened must nominate a doctor to whom the report is sent. It is the radiologist's responsible to ensure that significant results are communicated to a practitioner who will continue care. If a radiologist does a biopsy and there is malignancy he/she is responsible until the result has been definitely received and acknowledged by someone who will continue care.

entries.question: I am a postdoctoral researcher from the Health Innovation research group at the University of Cape Town’s Division of Biomedical Engineering, and I am currently working on a project that focusses on the market formation of the Lodox full-body X-ray imaging system. I am writing today in the hope that you might be able to refer me to one of your colleagues who is familiar with Lodox (even if they do not use Lodox) and would be happy to give us some further insights on its role in radiology. Any help would be much appreciated. Many thanks for your time
entries.answer: The RSSA has reviewed the Lodox system over the years and it was last considered in detail in 2016. Our view, and one shared by radiologists in practice in South Africa, is that the technology has no value in modern radiology. The RSSA does not support its use and has not developed a procedural code for it. In an acute trauma situation a combination of focussed regional digital radiography and whole body multislice CT produce a better and safer diagnostic outcome. Please call me if you need further information.

entries.question: 1. The MDCT register that Jako sends us annually. Are only the Radiologists on this register allowed to do CTCA/CT Colonoscopy or can they do these examinations, even if they are not on the register? What is the background and the purpose of this register? Are there specific accreditation criteria?
entries.answer: Original, and still to date, purpose was to protect public against unscrupulous operators from performing sub-standard studies on inadequate equipment and reports be issued by Radiologist with a minimum level of training. We require that at least one partner in a practice must have completed the necessary training and trust that all cases are at least discussed with this trained Radiologist. This system came about in part due to pressure from Disco to prevent a blow-out. I agree with the system. I know the follow questions and the answer is not. Not a practically

entries.question: I am in the process of restructuring our medico legal billing practices. Is there a recommended or approved rate for medicolegal imaging / reporting?
entries.answer: There is no recommended rate and the RSSA due to competition commission restrictions can neither recommend nor publish one. You should determine an appropriate rate from the work involved and the time to collect the fees. Historically radiologists have charged up to three times their normal rates for these services.

entries.question: Drs Bloch & Partners have purchased a MRI compatible anesthetic machine. What fee may we charge for the use of this unit? I cannot find anything under the existing code structures. Surely we are entitled to a return on our investment?
entries.answer: There is, to my knowledge, no way that a a radiologist can change an equipment fee for an anaesthetic machine unless the machine is used in a registered theatre. Several practices have had these machines over the years and have regarded them as a cost of doing business with their return coming from the additional work generated. I have attached a billing list for disposables used by one of them which may be of some help to you. As I haven’t researched this for some while, I will look at it again and get back to you.

entries.question: The partners have a query regarding lower limb ultrasound Doppler’s We have tariff 70230 for a Doppler. We now have a query with a patient that had both limbs done. Usually we charge code 70230 once as the description says “limbs” However the new partner seems to feel that there should be a way to charge for both left and right as we do with other regions.. I’m really not sure what the guidelines are. Would you please clarify this issue?
entries.answer: Baie dankie vir die goeie voorstel. Ons is besig om die kode struktuur op te dateer en sal ‘n nuwe bilaterale kode insluit in die beplande nuwe kode struktuur.

entries.question: What???s the process to follow for registering a new practice? Myself and a partner have recently started a new practice in a new private hospital.
entries.answer: Requirements are: 1. National Dept or Health Radiation Control license to operate each item of ionizing and MRI equipment 2. Depending on which province, may need a provincial Dept of Health license to operate. 3. Full HPCSA registration / licenses for the radiologists 4. Radiographers need HPCSA registration and if doing mammography accreditation (SORSA) 5. PCNS (Practice Code Numbering System) number in order to bill medical aids 6. RSSA accreditation for doing CCTA and Virtual Colonoscopy, and angiography procedures on own equipment

entries.question: Oncology referral, and my colleage called the practice to confirm if both T-spine and Pelvis were for radiotherapy planning and Clarissa from Lake practice confirmed that both the T-spine and Pelvis were for CT planning. Previous examinations as follows (and I stand to be corrected, but believe the patient had a PET scan in December 2013:
entries.answer: Muy suggestion would be if done at same time: 00310 plus 53310 If done at two sittings: 00310 and 55310

entries.question: I am a medical doctor and I have a medical surgery. I would like some more information regarding the use of an x ray machine. can a doctor operate on the machine, with training first.or a full radiology diploma or degree is required
entries.answer: Licences to operate X-ray equipment are only issued to trained and qualified radiation workers (radiologists and radiographers) or other practitioners who have training approved by the department of health. As there are no approved training courses available in South Africa, it is unlikely that you would be granted a licence. The Department of Health can be contacted at 021 9486162.

entries.question: Please could you advise - orthopod moaning at us to provide digital 2d templating software for hip and knee prostheses. Is it usual for radiology to provide this, or does the hospital? If we supplied it is there a tariff code to charge for the software (the ortho has to do the actual planning anyway). Is a very expensive thing to provide when the only imaging service is a pelvic xray!!
entries.answer: It varies, some practices supply it and some hospitals supply it. My view is that it is a service to the referring doctors and the spin off is CT and MRI. No specific tariff for it. You can negotiate with your hospital to supply it but it makes the hospitals case stronger to own radiology if radiologists try to push non profit items on to the hospitals.

entries.question: Do you perhaps have a tariff code or recommended charge if patients who had examinations done elsewhere comes for consult in our hospital and the new referring doctor wants to load and save the previous images on our PACS? (so something like PACS fee because it was not our patient)
entries.answer: There is no code for this, most practices will load and store as goodwill gesture to supporting doctor, if you produce a written report you may use: 01050 Written report on study done elsewhere, short 01055 Written report on study done elsewhere, extensive

entries.question: I would like to enquire regarding the RSSA rules and regulations or policies regarding private radiology practice. 1. Can a Radiologist partner up with anyone other than a Radiologist to form a practice? 2. Can a radiologist partner with a General practitioner? 3. Can a General Practitioner or other 3rd party invest in a Radiology practice? 4. Can the Radiology Practice have shares in a hospital? 5. What is the most common form of practicing in SA. eg Rental of hospital Radiology facility, hospital owns machines and Radiologist rent facility with equipment.
entries.answer: 1. Can a Radiologist partner up with anyone other than a Radiologist to form a practice? A radiologist can form a partnership with a Nuclear Medicine Physician or a radiographer. A radiologist may not practice in association, in a partnership, an incorporated company or in any other structure with any other healthcare worker, The relevant parts of the Ethical Rules and Annexure 6 of the Ethical Rules are copied below: (Full Ethical Rules attached) 1. Ethical Rules a. Ethical Rule 8 provides as follows: 8. Partnership and juristic persons.—(1) A practitioner may practise in partnership or association with or employ only a practitioner who is registered under the Act and who is not prohibited under any of the annexures to these rules or any ethical rulings from entering into such partnership or association or being so employed: Provided that, in the case of employment, the practitioner so employed either provides a supportive health care service to complete or supplement the employing practitioner’s healthcare or treatment intervention or is in the same professional category as the employing practitioner. [Sub-r. (1) substituted by r. 4 of GNR.68 of 2 February 2009.] (2) A practitioner shall practise in or as a juristic person who is exempted from registration in terms of section 54A of the Act only if such juristic person complies with the conditions of such exemption. (3) A practitioner shall practise in a partnership, association or as a juristic person only within the scope of the profession in respect of which he or she is registered under the Act. (4) A practitioner shall not practise in any other form of practice which has inherent requirements or conditions that violate or potentially may violate one or more of these rules or an annexure to these rules. b. Rule 3(2)(b) and (d) of Annexure 6 to the Ethical Rules provides as follows: 3. Partnerships and juristic persons.— (2) The provisions in rule 8 (3) shall be limited in that— (b) a medical specialist who practises in diagnostic radiology shall be excluded from the concession to form an incorporated practice in terms of section 54A, or to form a partnership or association with a medical practitioner, medical specialist or another practitioner who does not practise in the speciality diagnostic radiology (d) the only exception to the restriction pertaining to radiology referred to in paragraph (b) hereof shall be that a radiologist shall be permitted to form an incorporated practice, partnership or association with a nuclear physician or a radiographer registered in the relevant discipline, in view of the fact that the said professions are related to each other in terms of the nature of their field of professional practice 2. Can a radiologist partner with a General practitioner? No as above, a radiologist cannot practice with a GP if the GP Is acting as a General Practitioner. There is an exception to this, in common practice, the legality of which has not been tested, when a GP is employed to effectively to do the work of a sonographer in a radiology practice. In this case the radiologist must both sign off and approve the report and carry medico-legal responsibility for the examination. Not recommended as this is not allowed in the Ethical Rules and the radiologist could be at risk if a complaint is made. 3. Can a General Practitioner or other 3rd party invest in a Radiology practice? No, this is to prevent perverse incentives, with the moral hazard being the incentive for refering doctors to increase referral for financial gain. A radiologist may not form a business venture with an unregistered person ( unregistered with HPCSA) and may not share professional fees with an unregistered person. The HPCSA handbook on perverse incentives is attached for reference. 4. Can the Radiology Practice have shares in a hospital? Yes,a radiologist can but the Ethical Rules state: 24. Referral of patients to hospitals.—(1) A practitioner who has a direct or indirect financial interest or shares in a private clinic or hospital shall refer a patient to such clinic or hospital only if a conspicuous notice is displayed in his or her waiting room indicating that he or she has a financial interest or shares in that clinic or hospital and the patient is duly informed about the fact that the practitioner has an interest or shares in the clinic or hospital to which the patient is referred and the patient’s informed written consent is obtained prior to such referral. 5. What is the most common form of practicing in SA. eg Rental of hospital Radiology facility, hospital owns machines and Radiologist rent facility with equipment? No, the commonest form is that the radiologist rents the space from the hospital and purchases his or her own equipment. This is as everything stands at the moment. We are in a state of flux. The Health Market Inquiry provisional report is due out this month which may propose new regulations. We are also attending a HPCSA workshop next moth where the business practice regulations are being reviewed. Nothing happens very fast so I would use the existing regulations in the short and medium terms. Please contact me again if I can be of further assistance.

entries.question: May you kindly advise / assist in the following: Is there an RSSA regulation that states that a non- Radiologist/grapher /Physicist are unable to own shares or be a partner in the Radiology practice? The reason I am asking is because I have a situation where someone (a Nurse with Management Experience and Skills) informed me of a radiology opportunity and now herself and her lawyer expect shareholding in the radiology practice. I am not comfortable with this and I told them as far as I know, that was not allowed. Now the lawyer is requesting RSSA regulations regarding this or where all is stated that they or his client is prohibited. The matter is at the discussion level. No court is involved. I am not sure as to how to deal with this situation. I personally feel that there is an increasing interest from non-radiology community to be part of our territory. And to us this is a career that we have passionately choosen. I am kindly requesting your assistance or advise on how to handle this matter. I will be sad if I loose this opportunity however I also want to ensure that I comply with RSSA rules and regularions at all times.
entries.answer: We are in the process of reviewing the current legislation on corporate ownership of radiology and are expecting a detailed opinion, the contents of which we will share with members in due course. At present a practitioner is precluded from sharing fees under the HPCSA Ethical Rules (available at www.hpcsa.co.za ) and attached: 7. Fees and commission.—(1) A practitioner shall not accept commission or any material consideration, (monetary or otherwise) from a person or from another practitioner or institution in return for the purchase, sale or supply of any goods, substances or materials used by him or her in the conduct of his or her professional practice. (2) A practitioner shall not pay commission or offer any material consideration, (monetary or otherwise) to any person for recommending patients. (3) A practitioner shall not offer or accept any payment, benefit or material consideration (monetary or otherwise) which is calculated to induce him or her to act or not to act in a particular way not scientifically, professionally or medically indicated or to under¬service, over¬service or over¬charge patients. (4) A practitioner shall not share fees with any person or with another practitioner who has not taken a commensurate part in the services for which such fees are charged. (5) A practitioner shall not charge or receive fees for services not personally rendered, except for services rendered by another practitioner in his or her employment or with whom he or she is associated as a partner, shareholder or locum tenens. It seems however that the person involved is effectively looking for a finding fee for facilitating the practice opportunity. There would not be a problem with you paying this as a once-off fixed amount, unrelated to future turnover or profitability of the practice. The other alternative, as discussed would be to, employ the person, at a salary and benefits to be negotiated, as long as there was no shareholding in the practice and remuneration was not directly related to turnover or profit. Finally, you should ensure that the person offering you this opportunity is not an employee of the organisation requiring a radiology facility and that you are not involved in any form of bribery. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you need further information.

entries.question: I have a few questions with regards to partnerships, coming from different colleagues. Dr X wants to buy into an existing partnership, but doesn't have the funds to do so. He wants to pay for the partnership shares using money obtained from 2 different loans. Loan 1 is from a SOE which wants to support young black drs and loan 2 is from a pvt company, also providing funds to young black professionals to get them started in the pvt sector. The existing partnership wants to know if 1) will it be a problem that money is obtained from a SOE; and 2) what happens if dr X cannot repay his loans to these 2 companies? Would these (non-medical) companies then be able to claim his shares in the practice, ie claim profit share? Drs A, B and C wants to start a new practice but don't have the money to purchase the equipment. (They want to purchase a SPECT/CT as well as PET/CT camera). They want to approach non-medical companies for funding, where either these companies would own the equipment and they rent it from the company, or where the company loans them the money. Similar BEE situation. They are also asking how would they go about registering with RSSA as a PET/CT practice? As I understood from the RSSA meeting in December at OR Tambo, the DOCTOR has to own the equipment and at present it is not possible for another company / entity to own the equipment and lease it to the doctor? Is this just an undeerstanding, or are there actual laws prohibiting it? I would appreciate your input on the above matters.
entries.answer: In respect to the your questions, initial response: Dr X wants to buy into an existing partnership, but doesn't have the funds to do so….A practitioner can source funds from anywhere. The sensible approach would be to cede the rights to equipment purchased to the lender as collateral but not any share of the practice or its income. Drs A, B and C wants to start a new practice but don't have the money to purchase the equipment…. Will be covered in opinion.

entries.question: Could you possibly assist? I have been asked to look into this but have been unable to find any applicable information. We have had to employ an “Authorizations Clerk” who is employed to authorize all CT and MRI scans. Is there any tariff code available to Radiologists to charge for the service whereby we are obtaining authorization on behalf of the patient?
entries.answer: There is no code for this. Part of global practice expense.

entries.question: Ek wil graag by jou hoor of is jul dalk bewys van ‘n kode wat deur die radioloo gebruik kan word wanneer ons ‘n drip vir ‘n pasient moet opsit? Ek weet die kode 0206 word gewoonlik gebruik deur die verwysende dr’s? Ek glo ons as radioloo sal ‘n soortgelyke 5 kode nommer he?
entries.answer: The code 0206 is a four digit code and should not be used by radiologists. There is no radiology code for putting up a drip. Obtaining intravenous access for injection of contrast is part of the procedure and does not attract a separate code or get billed separately. There are codes for insertion of central lines but that is a different issue.

entries.question: I trust that you are well. Please assist me in the following: I have a referring Doctor who refuses that we give examinations/results to the patient. We have to deliver the results sealed to his Practice. Some of his patients are requesting the results for a second opinion. He refuses to give it to them – our Practice are now issuing duplicates for these patients. Is this acceptable as the patient pays our account? Can the referring Doctor force us not to give any results to the patient? Thank you for the assistance in this sensitive matter.
entries.answer: I have to make a couple of assumptions in the answer as I am not sure how you are practicing. I am not sure whether you give patients reports for their own records or you are using the patient as a delivery mechanism. The patient has the absolute right to access to his or her own report. The RSSA’s recommendation is that unsealed reports should not be given to patients routinely but must be given to patients when requested. The patient is referred to you by a referring doctor and it is not unreasonable that the doctor sees the reports first , if that is what he or she wants, as long as the results of the examination are given to the patient in a reasonable time. The are many cases, such as a first cancer diagnosis, where this approach is advisable. The nuance is about timing. However, if the referring doctor wants all the reports sent to him, the referring doctor must be prepared to give a copy of the report to the patient if requested or forward for second opinions. I am not sure whether your reports are on paper or analogue but if the former you could send the referring doctor two copies and request him/her to give one to the patient if requested. If digital the referrer must be prepared to print or further distribute the report to the patient. The referring doctor cannot prevent you from giving results to patients when requested. I think that one of your radiologists should discuss this with the referring doctor and come to a solution that suits both parties.

entries.question: Please confirm if Medical aids should pay for x-rays referred by a physiotherapist.
entries.answer: In general yes. A radiologist may accept referrals from any practitioner registered with the HPCSA. Medical schemes have rules which determine who may refer what. For example most schemes will not allow GPS to refer CT and MRI. The reasonable expectation is that a physiotherapist should be allowed to refer plain film X-rays and ultrasound.

entries.question: Kan julle asb net weer vir ons praktyk duidelikheid gee tot wanneer ons swangerskaps sonars mag doen? Ons doen slegs eerste trimester swangerskappe vir gestasie bepaling by ons praktyk, maar is nie seker of ons kan sonar doen tot einde 12 weke of einde 13 weke gestasie nie. Wat is die RSSA se beleid?
entries.answer: The RSSA position paper on this is attached, along with a communication from MPS and a suggested letter to referring doctors. Unless you have full indemnity from MPS, our recommendations, in summary, are: 1. You do not do any obstetric ultrasound examination for patients with clinical dates greater than 13 weeks, for any clinical indication. 2. You may confirm the pregnancy, report on whether or not the pregnancy is intra- or extra-uterine, estimate the gestational age and comment on the pelvic organs. 3. You should make the patient aware before the examination that no attempt will be made to assess foetal anatomy or foetal abnormality. A comment to this effect should be put in the report.
